caption.text: Imagine Clarendon Hill before it became a defined residential neighborhood—a landscape shaped by rail lines, freight corridors, and scattered industrial activity. In the late 19th century, this was an active industrial edge of Somerville, where movement, extraction, and production structured everyday life and tied the area to Boston’s expanding economy.
caption.title: Introduction

caption.text: Rail corridors were the backbone of this industrial landscape. They carried raw materials, finished goods, and workers between Somerville and Boston, embedding Clarendon Hill within a wider regional system of factories, shipyards, and freight-dependent development.
caption.title: Infrastructure in a Developing City

caption.text: On this land, industry directly transformed the landscape itself. Brick manufacturing and related industries extracted local resources, drawing immigrant labor and producing the materials that physically built the growing city around it.
caption.title: Tuft's Brick Manufacturing Company

caption.text: Even nearby institutions like Tufts were embedded in this industrial world. Early Tufts-related brick manufacturing tied the campus to local extraction and production, placing education, labor, and industry within the same working landscape.
caption.title: Tuft's University

caption.text: Beneath the visible city, infrastructure systems like pumping stations made this development possible. They supplied water, managed waste, and enabled both dense industry and early residential settlement to function at all.
caption.title: Mystic Waterworks

caption.text: The Mystic River and its reservoir system were part of this engineered landscape, shaped to serve the growing needs of industry and institutions like Tufts College. Water was no longer just natural—it was controlled, redirected, and integrated into urban expansion.
caption.title: Mystic River Reservoir

caption.text: This map reveals an earlier layer of the landscape, where the Millers River and surrounding basins shaped settlement and industry. Before being filled or redirected, these waterways defined how the area developed, leaving traces beneath today’s built environment.
caption.title: Cambridge, Somerville, and Charlestown drainage areas of Millers River and Basins

caption.text: This view of the river shows a working landscape, where water, infrastructure, and nearby development were tightly connected. It supported factories, housing, and the institutional growth of Tufts within the same system.
caption.title: Mystic Reservoir 1898

caption.text: Today, the building remains—no longer pumping water, but holding memory. Its reuse as housing marks a shift from infrastructure of necessity to infrastructure of preservation, still rooted in the lives of the community around it.
caption.title: Mystic Waterworks

caption.text: The Mystic Waterworks became the stabilizing core of this system. It ensured a steady supply of water to factories, rail operations, and growing neighborhoods, allowing the industrial city to function and expand.
caption.title: Mystic Waterworks

caption.text: Railways, factories, and water systems worked together as one interconnected infrastructure network. Rail enabled industrial growth, industry increased demand for water, and water systems made dense urban life possible—each reinforcing the others across Clarendon Hill and Somerville.
caption.title: Railway Development

caption.text: The introduction of street railways shifted this system toward mobility for people rather than just goods. The West End Street Railway Co. connected Somerville to Boston more directly, allowing residential development to expand beyond industrial zones.
caption.title: West End Street Railway Co.

caption.text: In this 1874 view, the Elm Street area appears before the arrival of modern transit infrastructure, revealing a more sparsely developed landscape shaped by early roads and nearby industry. It invites the viewer to imagine how this space, once peripheral to major rail systems, would later become a key site of connectivity, reflecting the broader transformation from industrial edge to transit-oriented community.
caption.title: Elm Street Station

caption.text: These bicycle advertisements from the 1890s reveal a growing culture of mobility and consumer life in Somerville, where local shops sold “up-to-date wheels” to a public increasingly interested in personal transportation. Businesses like those on Elm Street and Broadway advertised custom-built and modern bicycles, reflecting how cycling had become both a practical and fashionable way to move through the city.
caption.title: Bicycle Advertisement 1890s

caption.text: That legacy continues today in MBTA stations, which now prioritize connectivity for residents rather than freight or industrial movement. These stations support housing, education, and local development, anchoring the neighborhood in a commuter and community network.
caption.title: West Somerville Station

caption.text: Beyond industry, the region also developed social infrastructure like the Cambridge Almshouse. It provided shelter and care for the poor and included a smallpox hospital, reflecting growing needs for public health in an industrializing city.
caption.title: Community Infrastructure

caption.text: Today, that same site has been transformed into an international school. A place once tied to poverty relief and disease containment now supports global education and youth development, reflecting a shift in how infrastructure serves communities.
caption.title: Lasting Legacies

caption.text: This site marks the presence of the Little Sisters of the Poor, a religious institution dedicated to caring for the elderly and vulnerable. Its placement within this industrializing landscape highlights how spaces of care and charity existed alongside railways and factories, reminding us that community support systems were an essential part of everyday life even at the height of urban growth.
caption.title: Little Sisters of the Poor

caption.text: This image highlights the institution as a place of care within an industrializing landscape. It shows how spaces of compassion and stability existed alongside the surrounding growth of infrastructure and industry.
caption.title: Little Sisters of the Poor

caption.text: This map highlights Somerville Hospital as part of the city’s growing public health infrastructure. Its presence reflects the increasing need for organized medical care in a dense, industrializing environment, where hospitals became essential to supporting and sustaining urban communities.
caption.title: Somerville Hospital

caption.text: Compared to later maps, this earlier view emphasizes how quickly the area transformed, with relatively limited development surrounding the hospital site. It highlights the rapid pace of urban growth in just two decades, as infrastructure, housing, and institutions quickly filled in the landscape.
caption.title: Somerville Hospital

caption.text: This image shows the hospital as an active part of Somerville’s urban fabric, reflecting the growing importance of accessible medical care in a rapidly developing city.
caption.title: Somerville Hospital

caption.text: This map shows the Woodbridge Hotel as part of Somerville’s late 19th-century built environment, reflecting the growth of services and lodging alongside industry and transit. Its presence highlights how commercial and hospitality spaces emerged to support a growing population moving through and settling in the city.
caption.title: Woodbridge Hotel

caption.text: This image shows the Woodhouse Hotel as a key local stop for travelers, reflecting Somerville’s growing network of services and mobility.
caption.title: Woodbridge Hotel

caption.text: On Clarendon Hill itself, public housing emerged in the mid-20th century as industry declined. Built over this layered industrial and infrastructural landscape, it marked a shift toward residential stability and social welfare.
caption.title: Public Housing Project

caption.text: That stability was later disrupted by highway-era planning. The construction of I-93 and related infrastructure fractured neighborhoods, altered accessibility, and reshaped investment patterns across Somerville.
caption.title: Construction of I-93

caption.text: This newspaper coverage captures the moment when residents and local officials in Somerville actively resisted the construction of I-93, voicing concerns about displacement, neighborhood fragmentation, and the loss of community life. It reflects a period of intense public debate in which highways were no longer seen only as progress, but also as forces that could divide and disrupt established residential areas. Despite this pushback, the project moved forward, leaving behind a legacy of altered streetscapes and ongoing tensions between regional infrastructure and local community needs.
caption.title: Push Back on I-93

caption.text: This image shows local protest against I-93, highlighting community opposition to a highway seen as disruptive to neighborhoods and community life.
caption.title: "Bury I-93"

caption.text: This poster announces a protest march against I-93, showing organized community resistance to the highway’s impact on local neighborhoods.
caption.title: Depress I-93

caption.text: This image shows mothers directly confronting highway construction, highlighting how concerns over homes, safety, and neighborhood life drove local resistance to I-93.
caption.title: Safety Concerns

caption.text: These changes reduced the neighborhood’s cohesion, shifting Clarendon Hill from a locally connected residential environment into a more fragmented space shaped by regional traffic and infrastructure pressures.
caption.title: Mystic Bridge 1944

caption.text: This image shows ongoing redevelopment in a busy Somerville neighborhood, highlighting how public investment and urban change continued even after the construction of public housing, reshaping already dense community spaces.
caption.title: Continuing Development

caption.text: This image points to future development, showing how planning decisions continued to shape the landscape beyond earlier projects. It reinforces how public development leaves lasting marks, guiding how communities grow and change over time.
caption.title: Lasting Impact

caption.text: This image shows the present-day site, where redevelopment continues to reshape Clarendon Hill. It reflects ongoing efforts to update housing while building on the layered history of infrastructure, industry, and community that defines the area.
caption.title: Present-Day Development
